reasoning: The sole gate protecting the entire vulnerable code path is the `current_user_can('manage_options')` check inside `display()` (line 21 of class-connection.php). This is a WordPress administrator-only capability that cannot be obtained by an unauthenticated user or a low-privilege user (subscriber, contributor, editor) in a default WordPress install without a separate privilege escalation vulnerability. The value is never leaked, exposed in HTML, returned in API responses, or derivable from any predictable pattern — it is simply a server-side role check. The actual exploitation scenario further requires Omnisend's own remote API (https://api.omnisend.com/wordpress/settings) to return a malicious `exploreOmnisendLink` value (supply-chain attack) or a TLS-breaking MitM between the WordPress server and Omnisend's CDN — neither of which is within reach of a typical attacker targeting the WordPress site. Because (1) the gate is admin-only and not breachable by unauthenticated or low-privilege attackers, and (2) even a compromised admin session would only self-XSS unless combined with a supply-chain compromise of Omnisend's API, the real-world severity is LOW. The code defect (missing esc_url() at landing-page.html lines 20 and 100) is real and should be fixed, but it is not exploitable by default.
